name: Silicon; from modified Siemens process, solar grade; at plant
description:
This cradle-to-gate unit process dataset is for the production of 1 kg of solar grade silicon, with a purity of over 99.9999999 % (Woodhouse et al., 2012).
This unit process includes raw material acquisition; refining; transport; production and use of fuels, and electricity; ancillary materials manufacturing; manufacture, and installation of capital equipment.
category: 31-33: Manufacturing/3279: Other Nonmetallic Mineral Product Manufacturing

processDocumentation.timeDescription: The period spans from the publication date of Masson and Kaizuka (2019), the latest study used in Frischknecht et al. (2020), to the publication date of this last report, on which this model is based.
processDocumentation.geographyDescription: Frischknecht et al. (2015), which is the basis of Frischknecht et al. (2020) and therefore of this inventory, considered that the LCI built in Jungbluth et al. (2012), and which was partially based on data form Norwegian plants and other Western European sources (as mentioned in (De Wild – Scholten and Alsema, 2007) was appropriate to model US production of metallurgical grade silicon. Energy uses are based on reports referring to Chinese production (Mason and Kaizuka, 2019).
processDocumentation.technologyDescription: This unit process represents the production of solar grade silicon through the modified Siemens process, which includes four key steps: 1) Reaction of metallurgical grade with hydrochloric acid to synthesize SiHCl3 gas, 2) Gas purification through distillation, 3) polysilicon deposition in a Siemens reactor, 4) Crushing. There are additional steps for the recovery of reagents, which can be seen in full in Figure 5.6 of Jungbluth et al. (2012).
processDocumentation.modelingConstantsDescription:
Frischknecht et al. (2020) split the electricity use between “at grid” and “at run-of-river power plant” roughly 24-76 %, because “one of the most important North American producers mainly relies on hydroelectric power”. Here only electricity “at grid” is used, partially to represent generic US production, partially because currently it is not possible to select one kind of electricity production (i.e., hydropower) from the US Electricity Baseline. 
Frischknecht et al (2020) assumed a truck transport of 2000 km for the metallurgical grade silicon, and 100 km for the chemicals, and an additional 600 km train transport for the chemical. They also estimated a 100-year lifetime and a 1 Mt/year production for the factory.
Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD), Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD), and Total Organic Carbon (TOC), are three ways of measuring effectively the same pollutant. To prevent triple counting, COD and TOC are set to zero, but the actual amounts are given in the description.
processDocumentation.completenessDescription:
Treatment of Missing Environmental Data
Jungbluth et al, (2012) and Frischknecht et al. (2015, 2020) did not mention any missing relevant environmental flows. Jungbluth et al, (2012) however, mentioned direct emissions to air were not expected, and that water emissions were not known, and the values included are estimated based on data from MG-silicon purification. 
Treatment of Missing Technosphere Data 
Jungbluth et al, (2012) and Frischknecht et al. (2015, 2020) offered no information on cutoff criteria or flows that might have not been considered. However, Figure 5.6 in Jungbluth et al. (2012) indicates fumed silica is produced, and that oxygen is required for that reaction. Neither SiO2 appears as an output, nor does O2 appear as an input.
Mass Balance
The mass balance for this process was not calculated.
processDocumentation.dataSelectionDescription: The inventory present in Frischknecht et al. (2020) Table 7 used here includes new estimations (see Modeling constants above), data from Woodhouse et al. (2019), Mason and Kaizuka (2019), and their previous report Frischknecht et al. (2015), based on Jungbluth et al. (2012), and containing data from De Wild – Scholten and Alsema, (2007)
processDocumentation.dataTreatmentDescription:
The values in the LCI in Frischknecht et al. (2020) reflect the expert judgement of the authors and of the sources they cite, since none suggest a weighted average from different sources/reports.
Process data quality
Based on the information included in the “Data completeness section”, the data quality characteristic (DQC) "Process completeness" was given a score of 2 (60-79 % of determined flow have been evaluated and given a value). It is likely the original report went through a review process, but because it is not explicitly mentioned, the data quality characteristic "Process review" was left as default (No documented review) and thus with a score of 5.
processDocumentation.samplingDescription:
System boundary conditions
The system boundary is cradle to gate and includes direct emissions, material and energy use, transport, and infrastructure. 
Data collection
The inventory present in Frischknecht et al. (2020) Table 7 used different sources. See “Data selection” above for a summary of those sources, and the original report for further details.
Uncertainty
Frischknecht et al. (2020) included a standard deviation for all flows, which is repeated here.
processDocumentation.dataCollectionDescription: Emissions are taken from a 2002 Environmental Report, raw and ancillary material consumption is based on a dataset from 2007, energy use was taken from 2019 reports, and the transport was specifically estimated for Frischknecht et al. (2020) report.

sources.name: de Wild-Scholten and Alsema (2007) LCI PV
Woodhouse et al. (2019) Cost Si PV

reviews.details: It is likely the original report went through a review process, but because it is not explicitly mentioned, the data quality characteristic "Process review" was left as default (No documented review) and thus with a score of 5.

processDocumentation.intendedApplication:
Situation C2 - Accounting, without system-external interactions - The intended application is a purely descriptive accounting / documentation of the analyzed system excluding interactions with other systems in the LCI model.
The target audience of this model are LCA practitioners, the silicon, solar and related industries and the general public. The inventory is considered largely complete, except for land transformation activities. For that reason, it is not recommended to use this dataset when evaluating land use impact categories. The results of the National Institute of Standards and Technology’s data gap analysis were analyzed using the ISO 21930-LCIA-US impact assessment methodology, as well as Cumulative Energy Demand and a subset of ReCiPe 2016 impact categories.
The original application of this dataset was the identification of data gaps in the Federal LCA Commons (FLCAC) (see "Project" above). However, the intended application is to provide a cradle-to-gate inventory (A1-A3, according to ISO 21930) using exclusively processes currently available in USLCI or generated as part of the project mentioned above, and elementary flows from the Federal Elementary Flow List (FEDEFL) to enable the use of a fuller range of LCIA impact categories than those reported in EPDs. For data quality purposes, the intended application is to provide a US average inventory for the year of publication of the original source.
processDocumentation.projectDescription: This dataset was developed as part of the “Public Life Cycle Inventory Data Gap Analysis for Silicon-based Photovoltaic panels” National Institute of Standards and Technology’s Technical Note.
